@LYU2026 I think you would need `CAP_SYS_ADMIN` also there are few things which i think can help.

* Your version of `crun` is relatively old (~0.20 added some keyring updates) , if possible could you please update `podman` and `crun` to latest stable.
* Try running with `--cap-add CAP_SYS_ADMIN` or `--security-opt seccomp=unconfined --cap-add all` just to confirm we can narrow this down later.
* For first error please share output  of `/proc/self/cgroup`, `/proc/cgroups` and `/proc/self/mountinfo
` 

Also **if you don't need a new keyring** and you are blocked because of it you can disable it by
* Creating or check if any of these files are already there `$HOME/.config/containers/containers.conf` or `/etc/containers/containers.conf` and adding 
```bash
[containers]
keyring=false
```

Not being that familiar with Chome OS - it looks like you're running
Podman inside a container. Do you have any control over the outer
container, the one Podman is running in? If you can make it a
privileged container, that would be ideal - it gives Podman a lot more
of what it needs to run smoothly, and will resolve this error. If
that's not possible, the suggestion to add `keyring=false` from
@flouthoc will get you started, but I suspect it will only be the
first error you encounter.

You can find more details on podman-in-container in this blog:
https://www.redhat.com/sysadmin/podman-inside-container

FYI: https://github.com/containers/crun/issues/675

> Chrome OS set `security.syscalls.blacklist: keyctl errno 38` on parent LXC container.
runc work out of box, but crun needs to explicitly set `keyring = false` in containers.conf.

You’re misunderstanding it.

Here is how it works:

- Chrome OS host
  - Chrome OS guest VM (this is where LXC is already installed and you need to change its settings)
    - LXC Linux container (this is where you are in and trying to install LXC)

Open Google Chrome, press Ctrl + Alt + T to get the crosh shell, and run`vsh termina`. This give you a shell to Guest VM for changing LXC settings.

New keyring is not required, you can just turn it off in `containers.conf` (for podman), but without setting `security.nesting: "true"` (in LXC), rootless won’t work as it will not be able to mount `/proc` and a few other things.

Not sure what do you mean by out of control. Can you please describe your machine and environment? Is it a locked down edu Chromebook device?

If you really cannot change LXC settings, you should be able to install docker-ce and run containers without need to change any settings (docker is not rootless though).

So you’re not on a Chromebook but on Google Colab. The post I wrote is for Chrome OS on Chromebook.

Your environment does look like a Ubuntu container running inside Chrome OS VM. In someway this is actually similar to how Debian container was running on an actual Chromebook inside a Chrome OS VM. In that case if the Ubuntu container has restricted security settings (very likely) and you don’t have control to the Chrome OS VM, it would be unlikely that you will be able to use nested rootless container inside the Ubuntu container.

I think this is purely an environmental problem from the locked down LXC security setting of the Ubuntu container in Chrome OS VM, which user does not have control.

`podman` and `crun` are both behaving normally and reporting errors caused by security settings in this environment.

Although some of errors may not be critical and may be worked around with different configs. Based on my experience with ChromeOS and its security, there will be issues that you simply will not be able to solve unless you can change LXC settings for the Ubuntu container.

Thanks for comments in this issue and the blog post from https://ntk.me/2021/05/14/podman-in-crostini/ I got podman to work on ChomeOS 94 inside a bulleye container. Here are the steps I used:
1. connect to the host container with 'vsh termina' then run:
2. lxc config set penguin security.nesting true
3. lxc restart penguin
4. lxc exec penguin -- /bin/sh -c "printf '%s\n' '1000:100000:65536' | tee /etc/subuid /etc/subgid"
5. inside the penguin container, create a /etc/containers/containers.conf with:
```
[containers]
keyring=false
```

It works:

```
$ podman run --rm -it alpine
/ # 
```
